analogy: Think of "Motion and Time" through the chapter's own opening images: SCIENCE 156 Wastewater Story 13All of us use water in our homes and make it dirty. Rich in lather, mixed with oil, black– brown water that goes down the drains from sinks, showers, toilets, laundries is dirty. Have you ever thought where the wastewater goes... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: SCIENCE 156, 13.1 WATER, O UR LIFELINE, WASTEWATER STORY 157, 13.2 WHAT IS S EWAGE ?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Motion and Time is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that sCIENCE 156 Wastewater Story 13All of us use water in our homes and make it dirty.

The text explains that rich in lather, mixed with oil, black– brown water that goes down the drains from sinks, showers, toilets, laundries is dirty.

The text explains that have you ever thought where the wastewater goes and what happens to it?

The text explains that 13.1 WATER, O UR LIFELINE Clean water is a basic need of human being.

The text explains that let us make a mindmap of the many uses of clean water.

The text explains that you can add many more.) Clean water that is fit for use is unfortunately not available to all.

The text explains that it has been reported that more than one billion of people have no access to safe drinking water.

The text explains that this accounts for a large number of water-related diseases and even deaths.

The text explains that people even children walk for several kilometres to collect clean water.

The text explains that is it not a serious matter for human dignity?

The text explains that the increasing scarcity of fresh-water due to population growth, pollution, industrial development,mismanagement and other factors.

The text explains that realising the urgency of the situation on the World Water Day, on 22 March 2005, the General Assembly of the United Nations proclaimed the period 2005– 2015 as the International Decade for action on “Water for life”.

The text explains that all efforts made during this decade aim to reduce by half the number of people who do not have access to safe drinking water.

The text explains that there has been perceptible progress in the direction of the aim but still there is a lot to achieve.

The text explains that cleaning of water is a process of removing pollutants before it enters a water body or is reused.

The text explains that this process of wastewater treatment is commonly Clean water put to use Drinking

The text explains that it is like a transport system that carries sewage from the point of being produced to the point of disposal, i.e. treatment plant.

The text explains that manholes are located at every 50 m to 60 m in the sewerage, at the junction of two or more sewers and at points where there is a change in direction.

The text explains that in case you do not have a sewerage system in your locality, find out how sewage is being disposed off.

The text explains that it will help you understand the processes that take place at the wastewater treatment plant.

The text explains that 13.4 WASTEWATER TREATMENT PLANT (WWTP) Treatment of wastewater involves physical, chemical, and biological processes, which remove physical, chemical and biological matter that contaminates the wastewater.

The text explains that sometimes it may be necessary to disinfect water with chemicals like chlorine and ozone before releasing it into the distribution system.

The text explains that the biogas produced in the process can be used as fuel or can be used to produce electricity.

The text explains that the water in a river is cleaned naturally by processes that are similar to those adopted in a wastewater treatment plant.

The text explains that 13.5 BETTER H OUSEKEEPING P RACTICES One of the ways to minimise or eliminate waste and pollutants at their source is to see what you are releasing down the drain.  Cooking oil and fats should not be thrown down the drain.

The text explains that a very large fraction of our people defecates in the open, on dry riverbeds, on railway tracks, near fields and many a time directly in water.

The text explains that groundwater is a source of water for wells, tubewells, springs and many rivers.

The text explains that 13.7 A LTERNATIVE ARRANGEMENT FOR SEWAGE D ISPOSAL To improve sanitation, low cost onsite sewage disposal systems are being encouraged.

The text explains that septic tanks are suitable for places where there is no sewerage system, for hospitals, isolated buildings or a cluster of 4 to 5 houses.

The text explains that the biogas produced is used as a source of energy.

The text explains that large amount of waste is generated Vermi-processing toilet A design of a toilet in which humans excreta is treated by earthworms has been tested in India.

The text explains that it has been found to be a novel, low water-use toilet for safe processing of human waste.

The text explains that the human excreta is completely converted to vermi cakes — a resource much needed for soil.

The text explains that in the year 2016, the Government of India has initiated a new mission known as “Swachh Bharat” under which a lot of drives such as proper sewage disposal and providing toilets for everyone have been started.

The text explains that this is called sewage.  Sewage is a liquid waste which causes water and soil pollution.  Wastewater is treated in a sewage treatment plant.  Treatment plants reduce pollutants in wastewater to a level where nature can take care of it.  Where underground sewerage systems and refuse disposal systems are not available, the low cost on-site sanitation system can be adopted.  By-products of wastewater treatment are sludge and biogas.  Open drain system is a breeding place for flies, mosquitoes and organisms which cause diseases.  We should not defecate in the open.

The text explains that the government has laid down certain standards of sanitation but, unfortunately, they are not strictly enforced.

The text explains that you Mahatma Gandhi said: “No one need to wait for anyone else to adopt a humane and enlightened course of action.” must realise your responsibility in maintaining the water sources in a healthy state.

The text explains that fill in the blanks: (a) Cleaning of water is a process of removing ____________.

The text explains that explain why it is harmful to discharge untreated sewage into rivers or seas.

The text explains that explain the function of bar screens in a wastewater treatment plant.

The text explains that explain the relationship between sanitation and disease.

The text explains that find out the sewage disposal systems available to them.

The text explains that (c) The purpose of screening as the initial process.

The text explains that for more information, consult: Millennium Development Goals: http://www.un.org/millenniumgoals/ “Water for Life” International Decade for Action: http://www.un.org/waterforlifedecade/ http://www.cep.unep.org/pubs/Techreports/tr43en/Household% 20systems.htm An early engineering feat: Indus valley civilisation One of the ancient civilisations, Harappa and Mohenjodaro had perhaps the world’s first urban sanitation system.

The text explains that there was a separate room for bathing, and wastewater was directed to the covered drains which lined the major streets.

The text explains that (d) How is air bubbled through the aeration tank?

The text explains that (e) How safe is the water at the end of the treatment?

The text explains that (f ) Where is the water discharged after treatment?

The text explains that (g) What happens to the plant during heavy rains?

The text explains that (h) Is biogas consumed within the plant or sold to other consumers?

The text explains that ( j ) Is there any special effort to protect nearby houses from the plant?

The text explains that within the city individual houses, or groups of houses, obtained water from wells.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 13: Motion and Time

prelims_traps: Do not treat SCIENCE 156 and 13.1 WATER, O UR LIFELINE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Motion and Time; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Motion and Time through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use SCIENCE 156 and 13.1 WATER, O UR LIFELINE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
